title: What Does Prayer Mean to Me?
subtitle: A journal prompt set for people who are rethinking what prayer means in their own life
excerpt: A set of six journal prompts for examining what prayer means to you, beyond the form it took in childhood. The prompts move from what you were taught toward what the practice might mean or look like in your life now.

practice.whenToUse: Use when the word 'prayer' feels complicated or foreign and you want to think about what the practice could mean in your life now.

content.text: A journal prompt set for the word "prayer" as it sits in adult life. For some people it is loaded with the form from childhood that no longer fits. For others it has been absent long enough to feel foreign. The prompts ask what the word could mean now, stripped of the form it came in.
New to journalling as a practice? Read
journal prompts as practice
first.
The practice
Warm-up
What did prayer look like when I was a child?
Write the specific form: a posture, a set of words, a time of day, a place.
What feeling did it give me then?
Not the doctrine. The felt experience of it when you were small.
Closer in
When do I already do something that functions like prayer, even if I wouldn't call it that?
Moments of gratitude, of asking for something, of marking an event as significant.
What would I direct a prayer toward now, if the form didn't matter?
What would you say, and to what or whom, if no one was watching and there were no rules about the right way to do it.
What does prayer give that nothing else quite gives?
The act of directing words or attention toward something larger. Write what that gives, if it gives you anything.
What would a daily practice of prayer look like for me, in a form I could actually keep?
Specific. Two minutes? A sentence? A posture? Write the version that fits your actual life, not the one from childhood.
Where this practice comes from
Original to homemade.education.
